title: AI Model Components, Selection & Configuration
blueprintRef: Identify AI concepts and capabilities (40-45%) > Identify AI model components and configurations

questions.stem: What is a 'token' in the context of a large language model?

options.text: The smallest unit of text a model processes — words, sub-words, characters, or punctuation
A security credential used to call the model

explanation.correct: Tokens are the smallest units of text the model works with: words, sub-words, characters, and punctuation, each with an integer ID.

distractors.B: That is an API key/auth token, a different meaning.
distractors.C: Tokens are usually smaller than a full sentence.
distractors.D: A confidence score is not a token.

questions.stem: Why do LLMs use sub-word tokens (e.g., 'un' in 'unbelievable') rather than only whole words?

options.text: Sub-word tokens let a fixed vocabulary represent rare or unseen words by combining pieces
Sub-words make the model run without any GPU
Sub-words remove the need for embeddings
Sub-words guarantee the model never makes mistakes

explanation.correct: Sub-word tokenisation lets a bounded vocabulary cover rare/unseen words by composing them from smaller pieces.

distractors.B: Tokenisation does not remove hardware needs.
distractors.C: Tokens are still mapped to embeddings.
distractors.D: Models remain probabilistic regardless of tokenisation.

questions.stem: In a transformer, what is the role of the encoder versus the decoder?

options.text: The encoder creates embeddings using attention; the decoder predicts the next token from those embeddings
The encoder predicts the next token; the decoder stores the vocabulary
The encoder renders images; the decoder plays audio

explanation.correct: The encoder builds context-aware embeddings via attention; the decoder uses them to predict the next most probable token, one at a time.

distractors.B: Roles reversed and inaccurate.
distractors.C: They transform representations, not merely store data.
distractors.D: These are not the encoder/decoder roles.

questions.stem: What does the attention mechanism do when processing a token?

options.text: Weights how much each surrounding token influences the current token to build its embedding
Deletes tokens that appear too often
Counts the number of words in the prompt

explanation.correct: Attention assigns weights to surrounding tokens reflecting their influence on the current token, shaping its embedding.

distractors.B: Attention does not delete frequent tokens.
distractors.D: It is not a word counter.

questions.stem: What is an embedding in a language model?

options.text: A vector that encodes a token's linguistic and semantic characteristics based on context
The API endpoint used to call the model
A compressed copy of the training dataset

explanation.correct: Embeddings are vectors encoding semantic/linguistic meaning; tokens used in similar contexts get similar vector directions.

distractors.B: That is the deployment endpoint.
distractors.C: Embeddings are not a copy of the dataset.
distractors.D: Temperature is a generation parameter, not an embedding.

questions.stem: Cosine similarity between two token embeddings tells you what?

options.text: How semantically similar the two tokens are (closer to 1 = more similar)
How many tokens are in the prompt
The cost of running the model

explanation.correct: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are; near 1 = very similar meaning, near 0 = unrelated.

distractors.B: It is not a token count.
distractors.C: It is not a cost metric.
distractors.D: It says nothing about training date.

questions.stem: How does a generative model produce a completion?

options.text: It predicts the next most probable token, appends it, and repeats until an end condition
It retrieves a stored answer matching the prompt exactly
It outputs all tokens of the vocabulary at once
It randomly returns any sentence from training data

explanation.correct: The decoder iteratively predicts the next token, adds it to the sequence, and repeats until end-of-sequence.

distractors.B: It generates, it does not look up stored answers.
distractors.C: It predicts one token at a time, not the whole vocabulary.
distractors.D: Output is probabilistic generation, not random retrieval of training text.

questions.stem: Why does a transformer use positional encoding?

options.text: Because token order matters, the model needs to know where each token appears in the sequence
To encrypt user data at rest
To reduce the model's parameter count to zero

explanation.correct: Positional encoding tells the model the order/position of tokens, which affects meaning.

distractors.B: It is not encryption.
distractors.C: It does not change parameter counts to zero.

questions.stem: Compared with a large language model (LLM), a small language model (SLM) is generally best when you need what?

options.text: Lower cost and the ability to run in focused domains or on-device
The broadest possible general knowledge at any cost
To eliminate the need for prompts

explanation.correct: SLMs trade some breadth for lower cost and suitability to focused domains and local/device deployment.

distractors.B: Broadest general capability favours LLMs.

questions.stem: You need to convert text into vectors for a semantic search index. Which type of model should you choose?

explanation.correct: Producing vectors for semantic search is the job of an embeddings model such as text-embedding-3-small.

distractors.B: Image generation creates images.

questions.stem: A team needs a model tuned for generating and reasoning about source code. Which is the most appropriate choice?

options.text: A coding-optimised model (e.g., GPT-5.1-codex or Claude Sonnet)

explanation.correct: Coding tasks are best served by coding-optimised models like GPT-5.1-codex or Claude Sonnet.

distractors.B: Embeddings models produce vectors, not code.

questions.stem: Your app must accept both an image and a text question in a single request and answer about the image. Which model capability do you need?

options.text: A multimodal model (e.g., GPT-4.1 vision or Phi-4-multimodal)

explanation.correct: Handling image + text together requires a multimodal model.

distractors.B: Embeddings models do not chat about images.
distractors.D: OCR only reads text in images, it does not reason about the scene with a text question.

questions.stem: You need a model available to all (no special registration) optimised for real-time, low-latency, high-volume chat. Which is the best fit per the course?

options.text: A GPT-5 family model requiring registration

explanation.correct: The course notes GPT-4.1 is available to all and optimised for real-time chat, low latency, and high-volume production.

distractors.B: GPT-5 family currently requires registration.
distractors.C: Embeddings models do not power chat UX.

questions.stem: You want to create brand-new images from text prompts. Which model type should you deploy?

explanation.correct: Generating images from text needs an image-generation model such as GPT-Image-1.

distractors.B: Embeddings models output vectors.

questions.stem: Your scenario requires generating short video clips from prompts. Which model family fits?

explanation.correct: Video from prompts requires a video-generation model such as Sora.

distractors.B: Chat models generate text.

questions.stem: You are building an agent that must plan multi-step workflows and reason carefully, accepting higher latency for better accuracy. Which model family is most appropriate?

options.text: A reasoning-oriented model (e.g., GPT-5.x with adjustable thinking levels)

explanation.correct: Multi-step reasoning and agentic planning suit GPT-5.x reasoning models with adjustable thinking levels (speed vs accuracy).

distractors.B: Embeddings models do not reason or plan.

questions.stem: You need strong multilingual drafting of business reports with a good cost/performance balance. Which model does the course suggest?

explanation.correct: Mistral Large 3 is highlighted for multilingual drafting, business reports, and good cost/performance balance.

distractors.B: Embeddings models produce vectors.

questions.stem: For a well-defined, common use case where predictable performance and built-in compliance matter more than customisation, what does the course recommend considering before custom model selection?

explanation.correct: For well-defined use cases, prebuilt Foundry Tools offer predictable performance, built-in compliance, and faster time-to-value.

distractors.B: Training from scratch is rarely needed and costly.
distractors.C: Video generation is unrelated to the general recommendation.
distractors.D: Embeddings alone do not cover a general use case.

questions.stem: What is a 'foundation model'?

options.text: A large pretrained model providing general capabilities that can be used directly or fine-tuned
A model that only works after you train it from scratch
A model that runs only on a single device

explanation.correct: Foundation models (e.g., GPT, Claude, Mistral) are large pretrained models offering general capabilities out of the box, deployable immediately or fine-tunable.

distractors.B: They are pretrained, not trained from scratch by you.
distractors.C: They are not restricted to single-device use.
distractors.D: It is a class of model, not a config setting.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of leaderboards and side-by-side comparisons in the Foundry model catalog?

options.text: To help you evaluate and compare models on quality, safety, cost, and performance before choosing
To automatically write your application code

explanation.correct: Leaderboards and comparisons help you evaluate models across quality, safety, throughput, cost, and compliance to pick the right one.

distractors.B: They do not write your app.

questions.stem: What does the 'temperature' parameter control when generating text?

options.text: How creative vs deterministic the output is (higher = more varied)
The maximum length of the response
The number of tokens per minute allowed
Which language the model replies in

explanation.correct: Temperature controls randomness/creativity: higher = more varied/creative, lower = more focused/deterministic.

distractors.B: Length is controlled by max output tokens.
distractors.D: Temperature does not set the language.

questions.stem: What does the 'max output tokens' parameter do?

options.text: Caps how long the model's response can be
Sets the creativity of the response

explanation.correct: Max output tokens limits the length of the generated response, affecting token consumption and throttling.

distractors.B: Creativity is temperature.
distractors.C: Region is a deployment setting, not max tokens.

questions.stem: What is the role of the system prompt when deploying and using a model?

options.text: It is the developer-set instruction layer defining behaviour, tone, and constraints
It is the end-user's specific question
It is the model's billing account

explanation.correct: The system prompt is the developer-controlled instruction setting behaviour, tone, role, and guardrails.

distractors.B: That describes the user prompt.

questions.stem: What does the TPM (tokens per minute) rate limit control on a deployment?

options.text: The throughput/capacity allocated to the deployment
The number of users allowed to sign in

explanation.correct: TPM controls throughput and rate-limit boundaries: higher TPM = more capacity, lower TPM = slower.

distractors.B: Creativity is temperature.
distractors.C: TPM is unrelated to training date.
distractors.D: TPM is not a user-count limit.

questions.stem: Your application starts getting throttled as it approaches its TPM limit. Which mitigation is appropriate?

options.text: Reduce max_tokens and/or the number of concurrent requests (or raise TPM)

explanation.correct: Throttling near the TPM limit is mitigated by reducing max_tokens or concurrency, or by allocating a higher TPM.

distractors.B: Temperature does not affect rate limits.
distractors.D: Removing the system prompt does not fix throttling and harms behaviour.

questions.stem: Which of the following are the deployment type options described for a model deployment?

options.text: Standard, global batch, regional provisioned throughput

explanation.correct: The deployment type options are standard, global batch, and regional provisioned throughput.

distractors.B: These are not the Foundry deployment types.
distractors.C: These are storage tiers, not model deployment types.
distractors.D: These describe a release strategy, not deployment types.

questions.stem: You have a large set of prompts that do NOT need real-time responses and want cost-efficient bulk processing. Which deployment type best fits?

options.text: Regional provisioned throughput for lowest latency

explanation.correct: Batch (global batch) processing suits high-volume, non-real-time workloads cost-efficiently.

distractors.B: Provisioned throughput targets predictable low-latency real-time needs, not cheap bulk jobs.
distractors.C: Standard real-time is for interactive use.
distractors.D: A deployment is still required to call the model.

questions.stem: Why might you pin a specific model version when deploying?

options.text: To lock in consistent behaviour and avoid unexpected changes from version updates
To remove the need for an endpoint

explanation.correct: Locking a model version keeps behaviour consistent and avoids surprises when newer versions ship.

distractors.B: Version pinning does not change temperature.

questions.stem: What does deploying a model in Foundry primarily provide?

options.text: A stable API endpoint that makes the model available as a service
A new physical server you must maintain

explanation.correct: Deployment exposes the model as a service through a stable API endpoint (with compute allocated, config locked, monitoring active).

distractors.B: No certificate is produced.
distractors.C: You do not manage physical servers.
distractors.D: It does not auto-create a website.

questions.stem: You want consistent, focused, repeatable answers from a model (e.g., for extraction). How should you set temperature?

options.text: Temperature has no effect on consistency

explanation.correct: Lower temperature yields more deterministic, focused, repeatable output.

distractors.B: Higher temperature increases variability.
distractors.D: Zero max tokens would return no content.

questions.stem: Setting a very low 'max output tokens' value will most likely cause what?

options.text: Responses may be cut off / truncated

explanation.correct: A low max output tokens caps length and can truncate responses.

distractors.B: Creativity is temperature.

questions.stem: In Foundry, model evaluators (e.g., safety evaluators, groundedness, relevance) do what?

options.text: Detect and score issues, but do not actively resolve them

explanation.correct: Evaluators detect and score issues (quality, safety, groundedness) but do not resolve them automatically.

distractors.B: They flag, they do not auto-fix.

questions.stem: Which evaluation metric set is AI-assisted rather than a classic NLP metric?

explanation.correct: Groundedness, relevance, coherence/fluency, and GPT similarity are AI-assisted metrics; accuracy/precision/recall/F1 are classic NLP metrics.

distractors.B: Those are NLP metrics, not AI-assisted ones.

questions.stem: You need a model for general customer-support chat with reasonable cost and broad availability. Which is a suitable choice?

options.text: A chat-optimised model such as GPT-4.1

explanation.correct: General chat is served by chat-optimised models like GPT-4.1.

distractors.B: Embeddings models output vectors, not chat.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions help reduce throttling against a deployment's TPM limit? (Choose two.)

options.text: Reduce the number of concurrent requests

explanation.correct: Lowering max_tokens (A) and reducing concurrency (B) both ease pressure on the TPM limit.

distractors.C: Temperature does not affect token throughput limits.
distractors.D: Prompt wording does not address rate limits.
distractors.E: Changing model type does not address the TPM throttling issue.

questions.stem: Which THREE are parameters you configure when deploying or calling a model? (Choose three.)

explanation.correct: Temperature, max output tokens, and TPM rate limit are configurable parameters.

distractors.D: Cosine similarity is a measure of embedding similarity, not a config parameter.
distractors.E: Positional encoding is an internal transformer mechanism, not a deployment setting.

questions.stem: Which TWO models would be appropriate when you must process an image together with a text question in one request? (Choose two.)

explanation.correct: Vision-capable GPT-4.1 and Phi-4-multimodal can handle image + text inputs.

questions.stem: Case study: A startup is choosing models for three features. (1) A semantic search over their knowledge base. (2) A low-latency, high-volume customer chat available without special registration. (3) A feature that creates promotional images from text descriptions.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (1) semantic search — which model type?

explanation.correct: Semantic search needs vectors, produced by an embeddings model.

distractors.B: Video generation is irrelevant.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (2) low-latency, high-volume chat available to all — which model?

explanation.correct: GPT-4.1 is available to all and optimised for real-time, high-volume chat.

distractors.B: GPT-5 family requires registration.
distractors.C: Embeddings models are not for chat.

subQuestions.stem: Feature (3) create promotional images from text — which model?

explanation.correct: Creating images from text needs an image-generation model such as GPT-Image-1.

distractors.B: Embeddings models output vectors.
distractors.D: A chat-only model produces text, not images.

questions.stem: Case study: A team is configuring a chat deployment. They notice (1) responses are too random and inconsistent for their structured-extraction use; (2) responses are being cut off mid-sentence; (3) under load, requests start returning rate-limit errors.

subQuestions.stem: To fix (1) too random/inconsistent output, what should they change?

explanation.correct: Lowering temperature makes output more deterministic and consistent.

distractors.B: Raising temperature increases randomness.
distractors.C: Max tokens affects length, not randomness.

subQuestions.stem: To fix (2) responses cut off mid-sentence, what should they change?

explanation.correct: Truncated responses indicate the max output tokens cap is too low; raise it.

distractors.B: Temperature affects randomness, not length.
distractors.C: Removing the system prompt harms behaviour and does not fix length.
distractors.D: Embeddings models do not produce chat responses.

subQuestions.stem: To address (3) rate-limit errors under load, what is appropriate?

explanation.correct: Rate-limit/throttling issues are addressed by raising TPM or reducing token usage/concurrency.

distractors.B: Temperature is unrelated to rate limits.
distractors.C: Version pinning does not change throughput.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the difference between using a raw model versus an agent?

options.text: A raw model does pure inference (prompt in, output out); an agent packages a model with instructions, tools, and knowledge for task-oriented workflows
A raw model can use tools automatically; an agent cannot

explanation.correct: Raw models perform inference; agents wrap a model with instructions, tools, and knowledge to act in workflows.

distractors.B: It is the agent that adds tool use, not the raw model.
distractors.C: Agents are built on a model.
